text: The surgical team performs dissection along the inferior border of the pancreas using a left-hand bipolar grasper, which shows signs of char damage.
The surgeon attempts to control a small venous tear using bipolar energy, followed by a vessel sealer, but both methods initially prove ineffective.

The surgeon repeatedly attempts to control hemorrhage from a bleeding vein using electrocautery, but the efforts are visually unsuccessful, highlighting the importance of adequate exposure and the need for an alternative method.
The surgeon exchanges the vessel sealer for a needle driver and loads a 4-0 Prolene suture with a lapar tie into the abdomen. A simple figure of eight suture is demonstrated to control bleeding.

The surgeon holds the needle in the left hand, maintaining tension on the suture. Options include placing another laparoscopic tie on the needle end to secure the stitch or placing an additional stitch for definitive repair.

During the dissection to develop the portal vein tunnel, bleeding occurs at the GDA stump on the specimen side. The surgeon attempts to control the bleeding with bipolar electrocautery, but the bleeding worsens.

The surgeon holds pressure with a grasper in the left hand to manage significant arterial bleeding, while deploying a rescue stitch to control the bleeding. The area of arterial bleeding is clearly visible.

The surgeon places a figure of eight suture using the rescue stitch technique to control bleeding. Both arms are used in tandem to apply gentle traction on the suture while performing the second throw, aiming to minimize bleeding.

The surgeon encounters difficulty grasping the needle and uses the pancreatic parenchyma on the specimen side to stabilize it, maintaining tension with the left arm. An additional suture throw achieves bleeding control, and a laparotide is placed to secure the suture. The procedure is then converted to open surgery due to challenging dissection and anticipated bleeding along the portal vein.
